coredata.dc:description: Electrocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction (ECO2RR) is a typical small molecule conversion reaction, which involves multiple-proton-electron coupling processes. As a typical competitive reaction, hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) competes with ECO2RR for protons, thus increasing the diversity of reaction paths. In this work, PdCu alloy is an effective electrocatalyst for ECO2RR. After the combination of carbon dots (CDs) with PdCu alloy, the highly efficient ECO2RR on the surfaces of PdCu alloy turns to HER for the first time. The strong adsorption of electrons and protons by CDs altered the reaction path on the PdCu for ECO2RR, as confirmed by a series of characterizations and transient photo-induced voltage tests, leading to the switch of ECO2RR to HER. This work verifies the important role of CDs in the regulation of electron transfer and proton, realizes the interference of the multi-proton and multi-electron catalytic processes on the well-defined active center (or one active center), and gives us a new insight to design the reaction paths.

(d-g) Elemental mapping images of Pd (e), Cu (f), and C (g) of PdCu-CDs. (h) XRD patterns of PdCu-CDs, PdCu, and CDs. (i) The full survey XPS spectrum of PdCu-CDs.
XPS spectrum of PdCu-CDs, PdCu, Pd, and Cu. (a) High-resolution XPS spectrum of Pd 3d for Pd, PdCu, and PdCu-CDs. (b) High-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu 2p for Cu, PdCu, and PdCu-CDs.

FE of H2 and CO for PdCu-CDs and PdCu. (a) FE of H2 and CO on PdCu-CDs at the potentials of −0.7 V to −1.1 V. (b) FE of H2 and CO on PdCu at the potentials of −0.7 V to −1.1 V. (c) The histogram of FE of H2 and CO on PdCu-CDs. (d) The histogram of FE of H2 and CO on PdCu (The content of CDs in PdCu-CDs is 1 wt%).

LSV curves of PdCu-CDs and PdCu. (a) LSV curves of PdCu-CDs and PdCu in N2-saturated electrolyte (dotted line) and CO2-saturated electrolyte (solid line), respectively. (b) LSV curves of CDs in N2-saturated electrolyte (dotted line) and CO2-saturated electrolyte (solid line). (c) LSV curves of PdCu-CDs, PdCu, and CDs in 0.1 M H2SO4. (d) Tafel slope of PdCu-CDs, PdCu, and CDs in 0.1 M H2SO4 (The content of CDs in PdCu-CDs is 1 wt%).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots regulate the multiple-proton-electron catalytic process and switch carbon dioxide reduction to hydrogen evolution reaction of PdCu alloy electrocatalyst

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The switch from efficient CO2RR on PdCu alloy to HER on PdCu-CDs.

Electrocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction (ECO2RR) is a typical small molecule conversion reaction, which involves multiple-proton-electron coupling processes. As a typical competitive reaction, hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) competes with ECO2RR for protons, thus increasing the diversity of reaction paths. In this work, PdCu alloy is an effective electrocatalyst for ECO2RR. After the combination of carbon dots (CDs) with PdCu alloy, the highly efficient ECO2RR on the surfaces of PdCu alloy turns to HER for the first time. The strong adsorption of electrons and protons by CDs altered the reaction path on the PdCu for ECO2RR, as confirmed by a series of characterizations and transient photo-induced voltage tests, leading to the switch of ECO2RR to HER. This work verifies the important role of CDs in the regulation of electron transfer and proton, realizes the interference of the multi-proton and multi-electron catalytic processes on the well-defined active center (or one active center), and gives us a new insight to design the reaction paths.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The current energy and environmental issues have become increasingly prominent [1–4]. The well-controlled electrocatalysis involved small molecule (H2O, CO2, O2, N2, etc.) conversion is one of the most effective means to deal with energy crisis and environmental governance [5–9]. However, for various electrocatalytic reduction or oxidation reactions, a series of competitive and synergistic reactions occur between protons and other small molecules. Although great efforts went into the design and application of efficient electrocatalysts, the way to control the path in the competitive reaction process is also the key and difficult point to achieve highly efficient electrocatalysts.

In this field, electrocatalytic CO2 reduction reaction (ECO2RR) is a typical model reaction, which can convert excess CO2 in the atmosphere into fuel or value-added carbon products, such as carbon monoxide (CO), formic acid/formate (HCOOH/HCOO−), ethylene (C2H4), methane (CH4), methanol (CH3OH), ethanol (C2H5OH), etc [10–14]. Compared with other reactions, ECO2RR has its unique advantages [15,16]. 1) The reaction can be carried out under normal temperature and pressure; 2) The electrolyte can be recovered, and the whole reaction consumes only water and CO2; and 3) The electrochemical reaction system is compact and capable of scaling up. The small molecule conversion process of CO2 involves the multi-proton and multi-electron coupling processes, especially in the production of more complex C2 and C3 products [17–19]. The reduction of CO2 to CO or formic acid is a process of two electrons and two protons. The reduction of CO2 to methane requires eight electrons and eight protons, while more electrons and protons are required for more advanced multi-carbon products (C2H4, C2H5OH, C3H7OH, etc.) [20]. Meanwhile, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), as a typical competitive reaction, competes to capture protons, thus reducing the reaction efficiency, further increasing the diversity of reaction paths, and leading to poor selectivity of products of CO2RR. The equilibrium potential of HER is very close to those of many carbon products, which makes HER more likely to occur on the surface of generally untreated catalysts [21,22]. At present, the design of the catalytic active center of the electrocatalyst for CO2 reduction is mainly based on metals, such as alloys, transition metal-nitrogen-carbon single-atom catalysts (M–N–C SACs), metal organic frameworks (MOFs), metal carbon-based materials, etc [23,24]. Only a few non-metallic materials have the ECO2RR properties, such as nitrogen doped carbon dots, carbon nanotubes, and so on [25]. In addition, the regulation of the protons and electrons is very crucial. Cheng et al. [26] studied the effect of low frequency electrons transfer behavior on the conversion of CO2 to CH4 in Mn3O4/FeNbO4 heterojunction. Although some work have focused on the multi-proton and multi-electron catalytic process [27,28], there is few literatures on the regulation of this process by CDs. In our previous work [29], a cheap and stable three component electrocatalyst Co3O4-CDots-C3N4 was designed and synthesized, in which Co3O4 is the active center of HER, CDots are the stable center of active H, and C3N4 is the active center of CO2RR, realizing efficient, stable, and adjustable syngas (H2/CO) production. However, the regulation of the multi-proton and multi-electron catalytic processes by CDs on a well-defined active center (or on one active center) has not been achieved.

Alloying is an effective method to adjust the electronic structure, control the metal shape, and change the binding strength of intermediate substances to speed up the reaction on the catalyst [30,31]. Due to lattice mismatch and electronic coupling caused by different work functions, alloying can affect the d-band centers of active components, thus affecting the binding energy of reactant molecules on the catalyst surface [32]. The change of surface strain and binding mode of intermediates will also affect the binding energy of intermediates [33]. Alloys are usually the effective active center in catalysis [34,35]. At present, the noble metal catalysts are often combined with Cu, such as CuAu [36], CuPd [37], CuAg [38,39], etc., which can greatly improve the catalytic activity and product selectivity of single metal catalysts. Zhang et al. [40] adjusted the composition of the AgCu alloy by anodizing the Ag52Cu39Sn9 alloy foil. The change of the electronic structure of Ag and Cu modulates the bonding strength of the intermediate. In addition, the catalytic performance of the products can be controlled by changing the atomic arrangement of the alloy (order, intermetallic, disorder, core/shell structure, phase separation and high entropy structure).

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new kind of carbon nano materials, which are composed of highly crystalline carbon cores and surface rich functional groups. The size of CDs is generally less than 10 nm [41,42]. There are many defects and functional groups on the surface of CDs [43–45]. Therefore, CDs have rich optical and photoelectrochemical properties, such as excellent UV–vis adsorption, strong proton adsorption, electron medium, and well stability [46,47]. Because of its high electron mobility and the ability to quickly extract electrons, CDs can be used as excellent electron or proton carriers [48–50]. Besides, OH, COOH and CO groups on the surface of CDs are conducive to attracting H+ [51,52]. Due to its special properties and functions, CDs have been widely used in regulating catalytic activity [53–55]. In our previous works, CDs can effectively stabilize the active hydrogen (H*) and enhance the adsorption capabilities for H+ [29]. In addition, Zhou et. al [56] used CDs to change the electron distribution and transfer on Cu/CuO, and the catalytic performance was greatly improved. CDs can collect electrons and improve the charge concentration on the surface of catalysts. Based on the above research, CDs can be used as an excellent carrier for electrons transfer and have strong adsorption capacity for H+. These properties make CDs hopeful to become a key material for regulating the multi-proton and multi-electron catalytic process.

Here, we show that the behavior of active species (H+ and e−) could be regulated without changing the existing catalytic active center by adding catalyst components (Scheme 1 ). CDs as a co-catalyst were combined with PdCu alloy, and the reaction can switch from efficient CO2RR to efficient HER. At −0.9 V (vs RHE), 85.00 % faraday efficiency (FE) of CO can be achieved on PdCu alloy and the density current can reach −5.2 mA cm−2. However, when CDs were loaded on the PdCu alloy, the reaction was switched to HER, and 76.68 % FE of H2 was achieved at −0.9 V (vs RHE). The behavior of electrons and protons on the surface of catalysts was studied by transient photo-induced voltage (TPV) tests. The results show that the incorporation of CDs does not affect the catalytic active center, but changes the path of H+ and e- participating in the reaction. The strong adsorption capacity of CDs for H+ and e- makes them preferentially participate in HER. This work provides a new method for regulating the active species in catalytic reactions, which is conducive to in-depth understanding of the catalytic mechanism. At the same time, this is also a new understanding of the function of CDs.

Potassium chloropalladite (99.9 %, K2PdCl4) was purchased from Macklin. Copper (II) acetate (Cu(OAc)2·H2O) and ethylene glycol (98 %, C2H6O2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (China). 1-methylimidazole, (≥99.0 %, C4H6N2) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (China). Graphite rod (99.99 %) was purchased from Alfa Aesar Co. Ltd. Ultrapure water (resistivity: ρ ≥ 18 MΩ·cm) is used throughout the synthesis process.

CDs were synthesized by electrochemical etching method reported in our previous work [57]. Two graphite rods were inserted into ultrapure water as anode and cathode respectively, and then 30 V electrostatic potential was applied to the two electrodes using a direct current power supply. After 20 days of continuous electrolysis, black CDs solution was obtained. Then, the CDs solution were filtered and centrifuged to purify. Through freeze-drying, the concentration of CDs solution is calculated as 0.2 mg/mL.

The super-branched PdCu alloy was synthesized via a one-pot solvothermal process reported in prior work [58]. Briefly, 0.2 g Cu(OAc)2·H2O and 0.163 g K2PdCl4 were added into 50 mL ethylene glycol solution with 12 mL 1-methylimidazole. After ultrasounding for 20 min at room temperature, the prepared solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooled down to room temperature naturally, the product was centrifuged and washed with ethanol and water for three or four times, and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven overnight. The obtained black powder was denoted as PdCu. The pure Pd and pure Cu nanoparticles were prepared as discussed in the above method without the addition of Cu(OAc)2·H2O and K2PdCl4, respectively.

The 50 mg PdCu was added into 2.5 mL CDs solution (0.2 mg/mL), and the solution continued to ultrasonic for 4 h until the solution is clear and transparent. PdCu-CDs complex was obtained by centrifugation. PdCu-CDs-0.5, PdCu-CDs-0.75, PdCu-CDs-1.5, and PdCu-CDs-3 were obtained by adding 1.25 mL, 1.87 mL, 3.75 mL, and 7.5 mL CDs solution, respectively.

The PdCu was synthesized by a one-pot solvothermal process and the PdCu-CDs composite was obtained by continuous ultrasound. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) were used to characterize the morphology and structure of PdCu-CDs composites. As Fig. 1 a shows, the PdCu-CDs nanoparticles have a 3D sea cucumber-like shape with many branches. The average size of PdCu-CDs is about 500 nm. Fig. 1b is the enlarged TEM image of the branches of PdCu-CDs. Fig. S1a shows uniformly distributed CDs with average size of about 5 nm. HRTEM image (Fig. S1b) indicates that the CDs have good crystallinity. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is clearly observed, which is consistent with the (100) crystal plane of graphite. In Fig. 1c, the lattice spacing of 2.1 Å and 1.88 Å are corresponding to (100) plane of CDs and (200) plane of PdCu, respectively, indicating that the CDs are compounded with PdCu alloy. It can be seen from Fig. 1d–g that the Pd, Cu and C are the main elements in PdCu-CDs. C element uniformly distributes on the surface of PdCu particles, which means the CDs are well bound to the surface of the PdCu alloy. Besides, the SEM, TEM, HRTEM and element mapping of PdCu alloy are shown in the Fig. S2. The PdCu alloy has the similar 3D sea cucumber-like shape with that of PdCu-CDs. HRTEM image showed the d-spacing of 1.88 Å is consistent with (200) plane of PdCu. The element mapping suggests that the Pd and Cu are the main elements. Fig. 1h is the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of PdCu-CDs, PdCu, and CDs. CDs have a characteristic diffraction peak at 2θ = 26.38°, which matches well with (100) crystal plane of graphite (JCPDS No. 41-1487). The XRD patterns of PdCu-CDs and PdCu have no obvious difference, and both have four diffraction peaks at 2θ = 41.42°, 48.21°, 70.54° and 85.29°, which match well with the (111), (200), (220) and (311) crystal planes of PdCu (JCPDS No.48-1551). The diffraction peak of CDs is not observed in the XRD pattern of PdCu-CDs, mainly because the content of CDs is too small. Fig. S3 is the XRD patterns of PdCu-CDs with different contents of CDs. There is no significant difference after the introduction of CDs. Fig. 1i and S4a-b show the full survey X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of catalysts. The main elements in PdCu-CDs and PdCu are Pd and Cu. In general, CDs and PdCu were successfully combined, but the morphology and structure of PdCu were not significantly changed by the incorporation of CDs.

The high-resolution XPS spectrum was used to further investigate the surface chemical state of catalysts. Fig. S4c is the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of CDs, which has three peaks at 284.6, 286.2, and 288.9 eV, corresponding to CC, CO, and COOH bonds, respectively. The C 1 s of PdCu-CDs is also divided to three peaks of CC (284.6 eV), CO (286 eV), and COOH (288.3 eV) in Fig. S4d. However, the binding energies of CO and COOH show a little negative shift of ≈0.2 eV and ≈0.6 eV, respectively, which may be caused by the combination of CDs and PdCu alloy. Fig. 2 a and b are the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Pd 3d and Cu 2p. For the pure Pd, the Pd 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 peaks are divided into two peaks for Pd0 and Pd2+. The peaks of Pd0 are at 335.1 eV and 340.3 eV, and the peaks of Pd2+ are at 335.8 eV and 341 eV. Compared to the pure Pd, the peaks of Pd0 of PdCu are located at 334.9 eV and 340.1 eV, and the peaks of Pd2+ of PdCu are located at 335.7 eV and 341.3 eV. Clearly, the peaks of Pd0 and Pd2+ of PdCu have a negative shift of ≈0.2 eV, which suggests that the alloying affects the electronic structures of pure metals [59]. This may be because the electronegativity of Pd is stronger than that of Cu, and then Pd has more strong attraction to electrons. The same negative shifts also occur in the Cu 2p orbitals of PdCu. The changed electronic structure would affect on the catalytic performance [60,61]. However, PdCu-CDs has two peaks at 335.8 eV and 341.1 eV, which are the Pd 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 orbitals of Pd0. Due to the incorporation of CDs, the peaks show a positive shift. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu0 and Cu2+ in 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 orbitals also display the same shift. These results suggest that the CDs have the function of regulating the electronic structure of PdCu. CDs have been confirmed to have strong binding capacity with electrons [41], which can attract electrons from PdCu to CDs.

The electrocatalytic performance was carried out by constant potential electrolysis experiment in a three-electrode system and the products were detected by a gas chromatography and NMR spectrometer. There are only gas-phase products in the reaction system (Fig. S5). The FE of H2 and CO across the potential range from −1.1 V to −0.7 V of PdCu-CDs is shown in the Fig. 3
a. The FE of H2 increases from 62.96 % at −0.7 V (vs RHE), and achieves the maximum of 76.68 % at −0.9 V (vs RHE). Then, the FE decreases with the increasing potential. As shown in the Fig. 3b, the FE of CO on PdCu has the highest 85.00 % at −0.9 V (vs RHE). Meanwhile, the FE of H2 is only 17.00 %. It is found from the visual comparison of the histogram (Fig. 3c, d) that H2 is the dominant product on the PdCu-CDs catalyst, while CO is the main product on the PdCu. The FE results show that after the incorporation of CDs, the CO2RR with PdCu could turn to HER.

In order to study the catalytic active center and the reasons for the switching of reactions, LSV were carried out. The Fig. 4a shows that the density current curve of PdCu-CDs in N2-saturated 0.5 M KHCO3 is much larger than that in CO2-saturated 0.5 M KHCO3, which indicates that the HER reaction is dominant. At −0.9 V (vs RHE), the current density of PdCu-CDs in N2-saturated electrolyte can reach −27 mA cm−2 (red dotted line), which is 5.2 times of that in CO2-saturated electrolyte (red solid line). However, according to the plots of PdCu, the current density in CO2-saturated electrolyte is −5.2 mA cm−2 (black solid line), which is larger than −2 mA cm−2 in N2-saturated electrolyte (black dotted line). Obviously, the CO2RR is more likely to occur on PdCu. Besides, the initial potentials of PdCu-CDs in N2 and CO2-saturated electrolyte are −0.22 V and −0.51 V, respectively. In contrast, the initial potentials of PdCu catalyst are more negative in both N2-saturated and CO2-saturated electrolyte, which are −0.74 V and −0.63 V, respectively. The overpotential of HER of PdCu-CDs is 520 mV lower than that of PdCu. Fig. 4b is the LSV curves of CDs in N2-saturated and CO2-saturated electrolytes. The current densities of CDs in N2-saturated and CO2-saturated electrolyte are very low, which means that the CDs cannot catalytically reduce the CO2. Therefore, the CO2RR active center is PdCu alloy. As shown in Fig. 4c, the LSV curves of PdCu-CDs and PdCu are similar, and the current density of them is much larger than that of CDs in 0.1 M H2SO4 electrolyte, which indicates that individual CDs do not have well hydrogen evolution capacity. In general, the catalytic active center of both CO2RR and HER is PdCu alloy, and the incorporation of CDs does not change the active center. Fig. 4d is the corresponding Tafel slope of PdCu-CDs, PdCu, and CDs in 0.1 M H2SO4. The Tafel slope of CDs is the lowest (199.24 mV dec-1), which may be due to the strong adsorption of H+ by CDs, resulting in high proton concentration in the local environment of CDs [62–64].

In addition, the influence of different CDs content on the switch between CO2RR and HER was also explored. As displayed in the Fig. 5 a, with the increase of CDs content, the FE of H2 is increasing. When the load of CDs is 1 %, the FE of H2 is the highest, reaching 76.68 % at −0.9 V (vs RHE). However, the FE of H2 starts to decrease when the load of CDs continues to increase. Too many CDs may cover some active sites on the catalyst surface. In addition, excessive CDs would make the conductivity of the catalyst worse, preventing electrons on the electrode from transferring to the catalyst surface. Fig. 5b is the impedance circuit curves of PdCu-CDs, PdCu, and CDs at open circuit potential. The semi-circle radius of PdCu is the smallest (32 Ω), which indicates that the resistance of PdCu is the lowest. CDs have the biggest resistance of 43 Ω. The semi-circle radius of PdCu-CDs composite is between those of PdCu and CDs, indicating that the addition of CDs increases the impedance of PdCu alloy. In order to explore the reasons for the difference of catalytic activity between PdCu-CDs and PdCu, the electrochemical double-layer capacitance was calculated through the cyclic voltammetry curve. The double-layer capacitance of electrode materials was measured by single sweep polarography for catalyst samples. The capacitance current and scanning rate slope of the double-layer capacitance of different catalyst materials were obtained. The electrochemical active area (ECSA) of the working electrode is proportional to the C dl value, and the ECSA of the catalyst can be evaluated by the C dl value. It can be found from Fig. S6c that the ECSA of PdCu-CDs is much larger than that of PdCu. This indicates that PdCu-CDs have more catalytic active sites [65–67]. In order to prove the adsorption capacity of CDs for H+, the time-course adsorption experiment of H+ was carried out. As shown in Fig. 5c, the adsorption of H+ increased very fast during the first 15 min, and then slowly increased until 60 min. The maximum adsorption of H+ on CDs is about 70 mmol/g, which means CDs really have excellent adsorption capacity for H+. In addition, the adsorption of CO2 on the catalyst is the first step for CO2RR to occur. Therefore, CO2 adsorption experiments were carried out at room temperature. As displayed in Fig. 5d, PdCu has the strongest CO2 adsorption capacity, followed by CDs, and PdCu-CDs is the worst, which may be due to the biggest BET specific surface area of PdCu catalyst shown in the Table S1 (13.96 m2 g−1). The above results demonstrate that the adsorption center of CO2 is PdCu, while H+ is mainly adsorbed on CDs. The adsorption capacity of CDs for H+ may contribute to the switch of ECO2RR to HER.

In order to further study the charge transfer behavior between PdCu and CDs, the TPV test was performed. The device uses nanosecond laser radiation pulse (355 nm) as the external stimulus signal, where the platinum mesh covered by the catalyst was used as the working electrode, and the platinum plate was as the counter electrode. The schematic diagram of the test device is shown in Fig. S7. The typical TPV attenuation curves of PdCu-CDs, PdCu and CDs is displayed in Fig. 5e. The decay trend of CDs is slower than that of PdCu, indicating that its charge transfer rate is slower than that of PdCu. In order to compare the charge transfer rate of catalysts more intuitively, the time decay constant value (τ) was used to compare the decay rate (The bigger τ, the slower the charge transfer) [41]. The τ values of PdCu, CDs and PdCu CDs-1 are 0.31 ms, 0.36 ms and 0.54 ms, respectively. So, the electron lifetime on CDs is longer than that on PdCu, which means CDs can attract electrons. These results indicate that the charge transfer rate of PdCu-CDs decreases by nearly half compared with the PdCu because of the strong attraction of CDs to electrons. This phenomenon is also consistent with the positive shift of Pd and Cu binding energy caused by the incorporation of CDs in the XPS spectrum. For these reasons, electrons tend to be attracted to CDs and accumulate around CDs, resulting in higher electron concentrations at local locations. However, since CO2 is easier to adsorb on PdCu surface, electrons cannot participate in CO2 activation, and are more inclined to participate in H2 production.

In the end, durability tests were used to further confirm the catalytic performance of PdCu-CDs. As shown in Fig. 5f, when electrolysis was performed at −0.9 V (vs RHE) for 28 h, the current density remained at about −10 mA cm−2 with no significant decline. By contrast, the current density of PdCu only remained at about −5 mA cm−2 for 17 h (Fig. S8), indicating that PdCu-CDs catalyst has better stability than that of PdCu. Then, Fig. S9 shows the SEM images of PdCu-CDs after reduction reaction. The morphology of PdCu-CDs after reduction reaction has no significant difference with the original PdCu-CDs, except that the surface became rougher.

Based on the above experimental results, we described the path of H2 and CO formation on PdCu-CDs and PdCu catalysts, and explained the switch mechanism of CO2RR to HER. For the generation of CO: First, CO2 was adsorbed on the catalyst and was activated to form *CO2 – after obtaining an electron. Next, a proton was captured to generate *COOH. Then, *COOH obtained an electron and a proton to form *CO. Finally, *CO was desorbed from the catalyst surface to generate CO. For the reaction path of HER: Two protons get two electrons to generate H2. It can be found that CDs are the key factors for switching CO2RR to HER. As shown in Fig. 6 , the electrons distribution on the PdCu alloy are relatively uniform. When PdCu and CDs are combined, most of the H+ and electrons will be enriched around CDs. However, due to the weak adsorption capacity of CO2 on CDs, these electrons and H+ rarely participate in CO2 activation and were mostly used for H2 production. In the absence of CDs, PdCu has a strong adsorption capacity for CO2, and electrons can effectively activate CO2, thus completing the subsequent reaction to obtain CO.

In this work, CDs are proved to show the ability to switch CO2RR to HER on PdCu alloy. Original PdCu alloy efficiently converts CO2 to CO with FE of 85.00 % at −0.9 V (vs RHE) and the current density of PdCu alloy is about −5.2 mA cm−2. By contrast, the PdCu-CDs exhibits a low initial potential of −0.22 V and a maximum FE of H2 of 76.68 % at −0.9 V (vs RHE). Morphological characterization reveals that the addition of CDs has no effect on the morphology and structure of PdCu alloy. XPS spectrum suggested that the CDs capture electrons from PdCu. In addition, the PdCu alloy is confirmed to be the adsorption center of CO2, while the CDs are the adsorption center of H+. TPV also indicated that CDs prolong the lifetime of electrons effectively. These reasons for the CDs resulted in the switch of CO2RR to HER. This work achieves the regulation of multi-proton and multi-electron process on a well-defined active center. What’s more, this study highlights the unique behavior of CDs and provides insights into the design of functional switchable catalysts. In the next work, we will continue to explore the influence of different kinds of CDs or other factors on PdCu alloy catalytic performance
